judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are substantively equivalent, correctly identifying the same root cause, commit, remediation, and prevention measures with nearly identical specificity; Report 2 additionally notes the current stub test's exact weakness, but this does not affect coverage of the stated expectations.

report_1.evidence: In PostgreSQL, **table owners bypass row-level security policies by default**, independent of the `BYPASSRLS` attribute — RLS policies are only enforced against the owner if the table is explicitly marked `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`. Migration `0003_rls.sql` never issues `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`... see `juniper/.env.example:1`, `juniper/docker-compose.yml:5`, and `juniper/infra/main.tf:2`

report_2.evidence: In PostgreSQL, row security policies are **not applied to the table owner** unless the table is also marked `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` (which migration `0003_rls.sql` never does...). Currently, `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf` all set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper`

report_1.evidence: It became exploitable in commit `b2a23d2` ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot"), dated Wed Jun 17, 2026... `b2a23d2` changed `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf` to: `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper`

report_2.evidence: It became exploitable as of commit **`b2a23d2`** — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" (Wed Jun 17 15:20:00 2026)... `b2a23d2`'s diff changes exactly three lines, one in each of: `.env.example`: `juniper_app` → `juniper_owner`; `docker-compose.yml`... `infra/main.tf`...

report_1.evidence: Split the single `DATABASE_URL` into two: a migration-time connection using `juniper_owner`... and a request-time connection using `juniper_app`... Regardless of which role serves requests, add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;`... as a new migration alongside `0003_rls.sql`.

report_2.evidence: Revert the runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml` (`api` service), and `infra/main.tf` back to the `juniper_app` role... Defense-in-depth at the database layer: add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;`

report_1.evidence: Add an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = :tid` filter to the query in `juniper/backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19` (and any other tenant-scoped query) even though RLS should already enforce it — per project documentation's defense-in-depth guidance, isolation should not depend on exactly one mechanism.

report_2.evidence: Defense-in-depth at the application layer: add an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = :tid` to the query in `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, so a single point of DB misconfiguration (missing/bypassed RLS) doesn't unilaterally leak all tenants' data.

report_1.evidence: Add a boot-time assertion in the API service (as project documentation recommends) that the connected DB role has no bypass privileges and is not the owner of any RLS-protected table — e.g. query `pg_roles.rolbypassrls` and compare `current_user` against `pg_tables.tableowner` for `appointments` — and fail startup loudly if it's the owner or a bypass-capable role, instead of leaking silently.

report_2.evidence: Add a CI/startup assertion that fails fast if the runtime DB role has table ownership or `BYPASSRLS` — e.g., a smoke check that queries `pg_roles.rolbypassrls` and `pg_tables` ownership for the connected role at app startup and refuses to boot if the runtime role can bypass RLS on any tenant-scoped table.
